qa_pairs.question: Why do I have heartburn at 14 weeks?
qa_pairs.answer: This happens due to the hormonal and physical changes your body undergoes during pregnancy. It's usually normal, but discuss persistent or severe symptoms with your healthcare provider to rule out complications.

qa_pairs.question: Is pelvic pain at 17 weeks normal?
qa_pairs.answer: Many symptoms are common during pregnancy due to hormonal and physical changes. However, it's important to report any concerning symptoms to your healthcare provider. In Ghana, ANC visits are covered by NHIS, so don't hesitate to seek care if you're worried.

qa_pairs.question: Can I exercise with malaria?
qa_pairs.answer: Malaria is dangerous during pregnancy and can cause miscarriage, preterm birth, or low birth weight. Take your IPTp-SP preventive doses at ANC, sleep under treated mosquito nets, and seek treatment immediately if you have fever with chills. Prevention is key.

qa_pairs.question: Is back pain normal in the first trimester?
qa_pairs.answer: Some degree of back pain affects 50-70% of pregnant women, especially in the third trimester. Usually felt in the lower back. Practice good posture - stand straight, don't arch back. Consult your midwife for personalized advice.

qa_pairs.question: How do I know if I'm in true labor?
qa_pairs.answer: True labor contractions are regular, get stronger and closer together, and don't stop with rest. Go to hospital when contractions are 5 minutes apart, lasting 1 minute, for 1 hour.

qa_pairs.question: What should I pack in my hospital bag?
qa_pairs.answer: Pack by 36 weeks: NHIS card, antenatal book, comfortable clothes, underwear, sanitary pads, toiletries, baby clothes (4-6), diapers, blankets, phone charger, snacks, emergency money.

qa_pairs.question: What happens during first stage of labor?
qa_pairs.answer: Your cervix dilates from closed to 10cm. Early phase (0-4cm) has mild contractions. Active phase (4-10cm) has stronger, closer contractions. This stage takes 12-18 hours for first-time mothers.

qa_pairs.question: What is an episiotomy?
qa_pairs.answer: A surgical cut to enlarge the vaginal opening during delivery. Done if baby needs quick delivery or to prevent severe tearing. Stitched after delivery and heals in 2-4 weeks.

qa_pairs.question: How long does labor usually last?
qa_pairs.answer: First-time mothers: 12-18 hours total. First stage 12-18hrs, pushing 1-3hrs, placenta 5-30min. Subsequent births are usually faster at 6-10 hours total.

qa_pairs.question: What is a cesarean section?
qa_pairs.answer: Cesarean section may be recommended for various reasons: baby's position, placenta problems, previous cesareans, or certain health conditions. It's a safe procedure when needed. Discuss the reasons with your doctor. Recovery takes longer than vaginal birth but most women heal well.

qa_pairs.question: What happens immediately after birth?
qa_pairs.answer: Baby placed on chest for skin-to-skin. Cord clamped and cut. Baby assessed (Apgar score). Placenta delivered in 5-30 minutes. Try breastfeeding within first hour.

qa_pairs.answer: Your mode of delivery depends on your health, baby's position, and any complications. Most healthy pregnancies result in normal vaginal delivery. Discuss your birth plan with your midwife, but stay flexible as circumstances may change. Prepare for delivery by knowing the signs of labor.

qa_pairs.question: What is baby blues vs postpartum depression?
qa_pairs.answer: Baby blues: mood swings, crying for up to 2 weeks - normal. Postpartum depression: persistent sadness, hopelessness, difficulty bonding lasting weeks - needs treatment.

qa_pairs.question: When will my period return?
qa_pairs.answer: If exclusively breastfeeding, may not return for 6+ months. If formula feeding, may return in 6-8 weeks. Can get pregnant before first period.

qa_pairs.question: How do I know baby is getting enough milk?
qa_pairs.answer: Signs: 6+ wet diapers daily after day 4, regular yellow stools, satisfied after feeding, swallowing sounds, gaining weight, alert when awake.

qa_pairs.question: How to increase breast milk supply?
qa_pairs.answer: Feed frequently every 2-3 hours, ensure good latch, empty breasts fully, avoid bottles initially, rest well, stay hydrated, eat well, pump after feeds.

qa_pairs.question: What causes sore nipples?
qa_pairs.answer: This is typically caused by hormonal changes and physical adaptations during pregnancy. It usually improves as your body adjusts. Consult your midwife if symptoms are severe or interfering with daily life.

qa_pairs.question: When to start other foods?
qa_pairs.answer: Exclusive breastfeeding for first 6 months. Breast milk provides all nutrition including water. At 6 months introduce complementary foods while continuing breastfeeding.

qa_pairs.question: How to care for umbilical cord?
qa_pairs.answer: Keep clean and DRY. Do NOT apply anything - no ashes, herbs, alcohol. Fold diaper below stump. Seek care if red, swollen, smelly, or baby has fever.

qa_pairs.question: What vaccines does newborn need?
qa_pairs.answer: At birth: BCG and OPV-0. At 6 weeks: Pentavalent, OPV-1, PCV-1, Rotavirus. Vaccines are FREE at all health facilities in Ghana.

qa_pairs.question: Safest sleeping position for baby?
qa_pairs.answer: Always on BACK. Firm flat mattress. No pillows, toys, loose bedding. Baby can be in your room on separate surface. Keep room comfortable temperature.

qa_pairs.question: How to know if newborn is sick?
qa_pairs.answer: Danger signs: not feeding, fever or cold, fast/difficult breathing, convulsions, early jaundice, cord problems, very sleepy, constant crying. Seek help.

qa_pairs.question: Why does baby cry so much?
qa_pairs.answer: This happens due to the hormonal and physical changes your body undergoes during pregnancy. It's usually normal, but discuss persistent or severe symptoms with your healthcare provider to rule out complications.

qa_pairs.question: Signs of postpartum depression?
qa_pairs.answer: Persistent sadness 2+ weeks, excessive crying, difficulty bonding, withdrawing, appetite changes, sleep problems, fatigue, worthlessness, anxiety, thoughts of harm.

qa_pairs.question: How to manage pregnancy stress?
qa_pairs.answer: Get enough rest, gentle exercise, deep breathing, talk to supportive people, join support group, limit stressful news, do enjoyable activities, eat well, ask for help.

qa_pairs.question: Where to get mental health help in Ghana?
qa_pairs.answer: Local health facilities, MCH clinics, psychiatric hospitals, Korle Bu Teaching Hospital, Komfo Anokye Teaching Hospital, Accra Psychiatric Hospital, private practitioners.

qa_pairs.question: What is the contraceptive implant?
qa_pairs.answer: Small rod under upper arm skin releasing hormone for 3-5 years. Over 99% effective, removable anytime, safe for breastfeeding. Free at public facilities.

qa_pairs.question: What is an IUD?
qa_pairs.answer: T-shaped device in uterus. Copper IUD: 10-12 years, no hormones. Hormonal IUD: 5 years. Over 99% effective. Safe for breastfeeding.

qa_pairs.question: Family planning methods in Ghana?
qa_pairs.answer: Available: condoms, pills, injectables, implants, IUDs, emergency contraception, permanent methods. Most free or low cost. NHIS covers family planning.

qa_pairs.question: Traditional practices to avoid in pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: Avoid: unverified herbal medicines, vaginal insertions, tight binding, restrictive diets, using traditional treatment instead of medical care for complications.

qa_pairs.question: Is herbal medicine safe in pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: Always consult your healthcare provider before taking any medication during pregnancy. Some medicines are unsafe for your baby. Never self-medicate. Bring all medications to your ANC visit for review.

qa_pairs.question: Recommended traditional foods in pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: Kontomire (iron, folic acid), palm soup (vitamin A), groundnut soup (protein), garden eggs, beans, fish (omega-3s), pawpaw and oranges (vitamins). Eat variety.

qa_pairs.question: What is Group B Strep testing?
qa_pairs.answer: Standard pregnancy tests include: blood tests (anemia, HIV, syphilis, Hepatitis B, blood group, sickling), urine tests, blood pressure, and ultrasound scans. These are free under NHIS. Tests help detect problems early for timely treatment.

qa_pairs.question: How much weight should I gain?
qa_pairs.answer: Normal BMI: 11-16kg. Underweight: 13-18kg. Overweight: 7-11kg. Obese: 5-9kg. Gradual gain mostly in 2nd and 3rd trimesters. Focus on nutritious foods.

qa_pairs.question: Can I work during pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: Most can work with adjustments: regular breaks, stay hydrated, avoid heavy lifting, don't stand long. In Ghana, entitled to 12 weeks maternity leave.

qa_pairs.question: What is preterm labor?
qa_pairs.answer: Labor before 37 weeks. Signs: regular contractions, back pain, pelvic pressure, cramping, discharge changes, water breaking. Seek immediate care - treatment may stop it.

qa_pairs.question: How to count baby movements?
qa_pairs.answer: After 28 weeks, count daily. Rest quietly after eating. Should feel 10+ movements in 2 hours. If fewer, eat something, lie on side, count again. Seek care if still low.

qa_pairs.question: Pain relief options in labor in Ghana?
qa_pairs.answer: Breathing techniques, movement, warm water, massage, pethidine injection (most facilities), epidural (larger hospitals). Discuss options with provider beforehand.

qa_pairs.question: Can I breastfeed after cesarean?
qa_pairs.answer: Cesarean section may be recommended for various reasons: baby's position, placenta problems, previous cesareans, or certain health conditions. It's a safe procedure when needed. Discuss the reasons with your doctor. Recovery takes longer than vaginal birth but most women heal well.

qa_pairs.question: What immunizations during pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: Tetanus toxoid vaccine to protect you and baby. If not previously vaccinated, 2-3 doses during pregnancy. COVID-19 vaccination also recommended for pregnant women.

qa_pairs.question: Is back sleeping safe in pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: After first trimester, avoid flat on back - compresses blood vessels. Sleep on side, preferably left. Use pillows for support. Brief back sleeping is okay.

qa_pairs.question: What causes swollen feet in pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: Swelling is caused by extra fluid your body retains during pregnancy and pressure from your growing uterus. Stay active, elevate your feet, reduce salt, and drink plenty of water. Sudden severe swelling can indicate pre-eclampsia.

qa_pairs.question: How to prepare for breastfeeding?
qa_pairs.answer: Breastfeeding is best for your baby. Start within the first hour after birth. Feed on demand, usually 8-12 times daily. Ensure proper latch - baby's mouth should cover the areola, not just the nipple. Seek help from your midwife if you have difficulties.

qa_pairs.question: What is Braxton Hicks?
qa_pairs.answer: Practice contractions preparing body for labor. Irregular, don't get stronger, stop with rest or position change. Common from mid-pregnancy. Unlike true labor.

qa_pairs.question: How to prevent stretch marks?
qa_pairs.answer: Prevention involves: attending all ANC visits, eating well, staying active, taking prescribed supplements, getting adequate rest, and knowing the warning signs. Report any concerns to your healthcare provider promptly.

qa_pairs.question: What symptoms worry me at 8 weeks?
qa_pairs.answer: At 8 weeks (first trimester), common symptoms include nausea, fatigue, breast tenderness, and frequent urination. These are normal hormonal responses. Eat small frequent meals and rest when needed. Report severe vomiting or bleeding to your midwife.

qa_pairs.question: What to eat with gestational diabetes?
qa_pairs.answer: Gestational diabetes needs careful management to protect you and baby. Monitor blood sugar as advised, follow your meal plan, stay active, and take medication if prescribed. Well-controlled diabetes leads to healthy outcomes. Baby's size will be monitored.

qa_pairs.question: How is high BP managed at 32 weeks?
qa_pairs.answer: High blood pressure in pregnancy needs immediate attention. If BP is above 140/90 with symptoms like severe headache, vision changes, or upper abdominal pain, go to the hospital immediately - this could be pre-eclampsia. Take prescribed medications and attend all antenatal appointments.

qa_pairs.question: When to go to hospital if water breaks?
qa_pairs.answer: Go to the hospital immediately for: heavy bleeding, severe pain, water breaking, regular strong contractions, reduced baby movement, severe headache with vision changes, or high fever. For routine labor, go when contractions are 5 minutes apart. Don't delay if something feels wrong.

qa_pairs.question: What are ANC visit schedules?
qa_pairs.answer: Ghana Health Service recommends at least 8 visits: early pregnancy, then 20, 26, 30, 34, 36, 38, 40 weeks. More visits for high-risk pregnancies. Start before 12 weeks.

qa_pairs.question: What foods give iron during pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: Eat a balanced diet including: proteins (fish, beans, eggs, meat), vegetables (kontomire, garden eggs, okra), fruits, whole grains, and dairy. Drink plenty of water. Avoid raw/undercooked foods, excessive salt, and alcohol. Traditional Ghanaian soups and stews are nutritious choices.

qa_pairs.question: How often should I attend ANC?
qa_pairs.answer: At least 8 visits recommended. Start as early as possible, ideally before 12 weeks. More frequent visits in third trimester and for high-risk pregnancies.

qa_pairs.question: Can I travel during pregnancy?
qa_pairs.answer: Second trimester (14-28 weeks) is best time. Avoid long trips after 36 weeks. Walk frequently, stay hydrated, wear seatbelt correctly. Avoid malaria/Zika areas.

qa_pairs.question: What causes headache in the second trimester?
qa_pairs.answer: Headaches in pregnancy can be caused by hormonal changes, fatigue, stress, dehydration, or sinus congestion. Stay hydrated, rest well, and manage stress. Severe headaches with vision changes need urgent medical attention.

qa_pairs.question: Is garden eggs safe with sickle cell?
qa_pairs.answer: garden eggs can generally be eaten during pregnancy. With sickle cell, follow your healthcare provider's dietary advice. Some conditions require dietary modifications. NHIS covers nutritional counseling during ANC visits.

qa_pairs.question: Can twins affect my baby?
qa_pairs.answer: Twin pregnancy requires closer monitoring with more frequent antenatal visits and scans. You may deliver earlier than singleton pregnancies. Discuss delivery options with your doctor - many twins can be delivered vaginally. Extra nutrition and rest are important.
